trials.supervisor_analysis: Baseline established at score 60.20 with 74ms runtime. Profiling shows MIOpen LSTM dominates (99.28%). Multiple optimizations attempted in this trial. Advancing to optimization stage.

nudges.text: Writing a fully custom multi-layer LSTM in Triton is extremely unlikely to beat MIOpen's highly optimized `aten::miopen_rnn` which dominated your profile. Consider a more pragmatic approach: start from your already-passing baseline (score 60) that wraps `nn.LSTM`, and try `torch.compile` on the model or look for optimizations around the LSTM (e.g., fusing the h0/c0 initialization or reducing unnecessary computation since only `c_n` is returned). You've already spent significant time debugging correctness issues in a custom kernel that will likely be slower even when correct.

Don't settle for score 60. Try using half precision (`torch.float16` or `torch.bfloat16`) for the LSTM computation — the MI355X has much higher throughput for fp16/bf16 GEMM, and since GEMMs dominate 63% of your runtime, this could give a significant speedup. Cast weights and inputs to half precision, run the LSTM, then cast c_n back to float32 if needed for correctness checks.

Look at the reference `forward()` more carefully — it computes `self.fc(out[:, -1, :])` but discards the result, only returning `state[1]` (c_n). In your `ModelNew`, skip the `fc` computation entirely. Also, the reference calls `self.h0.to(x.device)` every forward pass — pre-move h0/c0 to CUDA in `__init__` or on first call and cache them to avoid repeated `.to()` overhead. These are small but free wins that compound over benchmark iterations.

Try wrapping the LSTM forward pass in a CUDA graph (`torch.cuda.CUDAGraph`) — with 6 layers × 512 timesteps, there are thousands of kernel launches, and CUDA graphs eliminate per-launch overhead. Do a warmup run first, then capture the graph, and replay it on each subsequent call. Make sure h0/c0 and the input tensor are allocated once and reused as the graph's input buffers.

Wrap the forward pass with `@torch.inference_mode()` decorator — this eliminates autograd tracking overhead (version counting, grad metadata) for every tensor operation, which adds up across 6 layers × 512 timesteps. This is a free speedup for inference-only workloads.
